title: Finding and Using Biological Databases
text:
Welcome
Modern biology runs on public databases. Before you can analyse a genome, quantify a transcriptome, or reason about a protein’s structure, you have to find the data — and finding it means knowing your way around the handful of big public archives that the whole field depends on: the NCBI/GEO/SRA family, the European databases at EMBL-EBI (ENA, Ensembl, PDBe), and the structural prediction and comparison tools that sit alongside them.
This short book is a set of hands-on, self-checking tutorials that walk you through real database tasks and let you confirm you got the right answer as you go. Each chapter takes a genuine biological question, points you at the right database, and has you navigate it for yourself — retrieving accessions, downloading data, and interpreting what you find. Little multiple-choice checkpoints along the way let you make sure you’re on track before moving on.
There are three tutorials:
crumbs: Welcome

title: Finding and Using Biological Databases
text:
How to use these tutorials
Work through each chapter with the relevant database open in another browser tab, and actually do the steps — the whole point is to build the muscle memory of navigating these resources yourself. When you reach a question, have a go before you reveal the answer. Where a question has a “Why?” note attached, open it once you’ve answered: it explains why the answer is what it is, which is usually more useful than the answer itself.
You don’t need to install anything or run any code to use this book — everything runs in your browser. The command-line tools mentioned in the tutorials (for downloading and processing data) are things you’d run on your own machine in a real analysis; here they’re shown so you can see the whole pipeline, and the questions are answerable from the information the databases give you.
Let’s get started.
crumbs: Welcome

title: 1  Gene expression databases and expression analysis
text:
1.1 Aims
The aim of this tutorial is to examine how we can use public genomics databases to retrieve RNAseq data that describes expression of genes. We shall then work through an example in which we carry out expression estimates for the data we downloaded.
crumbs: 1  Gene expression databases and expression analysis

title: 1  Gene expression databases and expression analysis
text:
1.2 Browsing for expression data
The Gene Expression Omnibus is a great place to search for gene expression data. There is a general ‘advanced’ search page at https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/gds/advanced/ which allows you to build a complex search. We’ll skip past that and jump straight to a given GEO accession. This is simply because we want to use a small dataset that will run quickly.
We’ll use a simple E. coli dataset that examines gene expression at different points of the growth phase in culture, one sample at 8h (mid logarithmic growth) and one sample at 14h (late logarithmic growth).
Use the GEO Advanced Search to find records relating to the accession GSE77674.

How many samples are available for the Series accession record we used?




 ✗1


 ✓2


 ✗3











Why?





A GEO Series (GSE) record groups all the samples in an experiment; this one compares two growth-phase time points, so it holds exactly two Sample (GSM) records — one for the 8h culture and one for the 14h culture.



Now that we found the record for the accession we can see the metadata about the experiment submitted, in particular the sample information.

What sequencing machine was used?




 ✗454


 ✓Illumina Hiseq


 ✗Nanopore











Why?





The platform is recorded in each sample’s metadata; here it lists an Illumina HiSeq instrument. Knowing the platform matters because read length and error profile differ between 454, Illumina and Nanopore, and that shapes how you process the reads.





Which sample accession describes the 8h reads?




 ✓GSM2055958


 ✗GSM2055959


 ✗SRP069289











Why?





Individual samples in GEO carry GSM accessions, so GSM2055958 (the 8h sample) is the right form of ID. SRP069289 is an SRA Study accession — it names the whole sequencing project in SRA, not a single sample.





What strain of E. coli was used?




 ✗DH5a


 ✗W3110


 ✓K12











Why?





The sample metadata names the K-12 laboratory strain of E. coli. DH5a and W3110 are other common lab strains, but they are not the one recorded for this experiment.




Use the NCBI taxonomy browser to find the taxonomy ID of E. coli K12 at the strain level. Which is it?




 ✓83333


 ✗51145


 ✗329











Why?





NCBI Taxonomy assigns a distinct ID at each rank, and 83333 is the node for E. coli K-12 at the strain level (the species E. coli itself is 562). Searching at the right rank ensures you pull sequences for the specific strain rather than the whole species.



In GEO/SRA ‘runs’ describe a single run of the sequencing machine and usually describe a single set of reads (sometimes paired).

How many replicates are done for each sample?




 ✓1


 ✗2


 ✗3











Why?





Each time point here has a single run, so there is one replicate per sample. That’s unusual and statistically weak for real work, but it keeps this demonstration dataset small and quick to process.





What is the run id for the 8h sample? (You may need to browse some of the links to find this out)




 ✗SRR3144643


 ✗SRX1560781


 ✓SRR3144708











Why?





Actual sequence reads live under a Run accession, which starts with SRR, so SRR3144708 is what you download for the 8h sample. SRX1560781 is an Experiment (SRX) accession — it describes the library, not the run of reads.




What is the run id for the 14h sample? (You may need to browse some of the links to find this out)




 ✓SRR3144643


 ✗SRX1560781


 ✗SRR3144708











Why?





The 14h reads are stored under Run accession SRR3144643. As before, the SRX accession names the experiment/library rather than the set of reads you actually retrieve.



Now that we have identified the run accessions we are able to download the actual reads to work on them.
crumbs: 1  Gene expression databases and expression analysis

title: 1  Gene expression databases and expression analysis
text:
1.3 Downloading data with SRAtools
The web page provides a download button for the data, and while for this small transcriptome with small files (relatively speaking) downloading through the browser might work, we can do this better using the SRAtools package on the command-line.
Start up your terminal program. We can use the SRAtools package to retrieve data in one step. We use the fastq-dump command to prepare the data and download it to an uncompressed text file in fastq format. Note that often you will not want uncompressed data as raw read data can be very large. You can check the SRAtools manuals for more flexible download options here.
Download the reads for each run e.g. — with ID SRR3144643 as follows:
fastq-dump SRR3144643
Make sure you download runs for both 8h and 14h samples. Use the run and sample name information you found earlier.

How many reads were downloaded for 14h?




 ✗9


 ✓9,339,062


 ✗212,897











Why?





fastq-dump reports how many reads (‘spots’) it wrote, and for the 14h run (SRR3144643) that total is 9,339,062. This count is a property of the run itself, so anyone downloading the same accession gets the same number.





How many reads were downloaded for 8h?




 ✓10,691,872


 ✗9,339,062


 ✗3,144,643











Why?





The 8h run (SRR3144708) contains 10,691,872 reads. Note that 9,339,062 is the count for the 14h run, so it’s a tempting but wrong distractor here.



Now that we have the reads, we need the definitive sequences of the transcripts. This will allow us to align the reads to the transcripts, count how many reads align to each and get a measure of the abundance of each transcript in the sample.
Databases like EnsemblBacteria (https://bacteria.ensembl.org) hold the resources we need. Go there and use the Search for a genome box to find Escherichia coli.

How many E. coli genomes are found?




 ✗25


 ✓about 1,200


 ✗about 31000











Why?





E. coli is one of the most heavily sequenced organisms, so a search returns on the order of a thousand assemblies — far more than a single reference. The exact figure grows over time as new assemblies are deposited.



Use the filter to find the genomes coming from the correct strain with the taxid you found.

How many E. coli K12 genomes are found?




 ✓about 26


 ✗about 1,200


 ✗about 31000











Why?





Filtering by the K-12 strain taxon ID narrows the list from all E. coli assemblies down to the couple of dozen that belong to that strain. Filtering on a stable taxid is the reliable way to pick out the strain you actually want.



There are so many results because numerous different sequencing projects have used this organism so there are lots of assemblies of the strains and substrains of E. coli. For our purposes we can select any of the assemblies but the differences may be important for your real lab work. Click the link in the species column of the top result (with assembly accession gca_004802935) to go to the page for that assembly.
In the Gene annotation box, select the FASTA link in the Download genes section to open up a link to the files containing the transcript sequences. The sequences of the transcripts are in a folder called cds (for coding sequence). Open that and copy the file called Escherichia_coli_k_12_gca_004802935.ASM480293v1.cds.all.fa.gz to the same place you downloaded the reads to. Using drag and drop is fine, as this file should be very small.
The file extension .gz indicates this file is compressed with gzip. On some systems you can uncompress it with a double click of the icon or you can do it on the command line like this
gunzip Escherichia_coli_k_12_gca_004802935.ASM480293v1.cds.all.fa.gz
crumbs: 1  Gene expression databases and expression analysis

title: 1  Gene expression databases and expression analysis
text:
1.4 Estimating transcript counts with kallisto
We can now perform a quantification of the transcripts using the reads. We only have one sample for each time point, which is highly unusual but is enough for demonstration purposes.
We’ll use the kallisto quantifier https://pachterlab.github.io/kallisto/about. The program works in two steps, we first make an index of our transcript sequences and then we pseudoalign reads to the index to work out how many reads come from each transcript.

1.4.1 Building the index
The index needs only be built once, we can do that with this command line
kallisto index -i ecoli_index.idx Escherichia_coli_k_12_gca_004802935.ASM480293v1.cds.all.fa
which only takes a moment for this small genome. The command indicates that we want to index and what our index should be called with -i, our output index is therefore ecoli_index.idx. The final argument is the file of transcript sequences that we wish to use.


1.4.2 Quantification
Now we can estimate the transcript abundances, once for each file of sample reads. We treat the file as if single-ended, though better quantification can be achieved if you have paired end info.
For the 8h reads we can use this command line
kallisto quant -i ecoli_index.idx -o 8h -b 100 --single -l 180 -s 20 SRR3144708.fastq
which runs quant using index -i ecoli_index.idx, the results will be output to a folder called 8h (-o 8h). One hundred bootstrap repetitions are performed (-b 100), single end mode is used (--single) and the length (-l 180) and SD of fragments (-s 20), is given.
The corresponding command line for the 14h reads is as follows
kallisto quant -i ecoli_index.idx -o 14h -b 100 --single -l 180 -s 20 SRR3144643.fastq


1.4.3 Examining abundances.tsv
The transcript abundances are placed in the output folder inside a file called abundances.tsv. Check it like this (remembering to use q to quit less)
head 8h/abundance.tsv
part of which looks like this
target_id   length    eff_length    est_counts    tpm
THH56748    1158      979           41            2.28274
THH56492    933       754           173           12.5063
THH56493    756       577           546           51.5789
THH56494    1248        1069          10            0.509891
We can see the target ID (the transcript name) and the estimates, resulting in the tags per million tpm at the end of each row. The tpm is our final expression estimate for the transcripts and is a value we can build into bigger exploratory analyses.
crumbs: 1  Gene expression databases and expression analysis

title: 1  Gene expression databases and expression analysis
text:
1.5 Wrap up and next steps
This tutorial is focussed on using databases to find reads and quantify transcript abundances and we’ve done that successfully. The process of RNAseq and transcriptome analysis once we’ve got those reads is much more involved. Analysing the resulting transcript counts to make valid and useful biological inferences is a very different topic that takes in statistics and data visualisation. There are a great number of introductory tutorials on all aspects of expression analysis all over the web. One good starting place is with kallisto’s companion tool, sleuth which will allow you to perform differential analysis and some important QC measures. Try here https://pachterlab.github.io/sleuth_walkthroughs/trapnell/analysis.html for a good introduction to sleuth.
crumbs: 1  Gene expression databases and expression analysis

title: 2  Genomic databases and genome assembly
text:
2.1 Aims
The aim of this tutorial is to examine how we can use public genomics databases, cross reference between them and download data for our own analyses. We shall work through an example in which we seek and retrieve data for an interesting species and learn what tools we have for downloading and processing data.
We will then go on to assemble and annotate a genome assembly with a basic pipeline.
crumbs: 2  Genomic databases and genome assembly

title: 2  Genomic databases and genome assembly
text:
2.2 Glossary

Sequence assembly: process of aligning and merging fragments of sequences to reconstruct longer sequences called contiguous sequences (a.k.a ‘contigs’).
Contigs: a contiguous sequences generated during sequence assembly.
Scaffolds: sets of oriented, sorted and ordered contigs.
Genome assembly: representation of the complete genomic sequence of an organism. A genome assembly is the ultimate result of the sequence assembly process.

NB: the terms ‘contigs’ and ‘scaffolds’ are often used interchangeably, however scaffolds contain gaps while contigs do not.
crumbs: 2  Genomic databases and genome assembly

title: 2  Genomic databases and genome assembly
text:
2.3 Browsing for information
Let’s use a combination of the GenBank/NCBI databases and the ENA to find out information and data for Campylobacter concisus https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Campylobacter_concisus
We’ll first make sure we know exactly which organism we are talking about and learn how the databases identify species. Each taxon known to science has a unique ID number at the NCBI Taxonomy Database https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/taxonomy. This unique ID number is the “taxonomic identifier’ (a.k.a ‘taxon ID’, ‘tax ID’ or ‘taxid’).
Use the NCBI Taxonomy Browser to find the taxon ID for C. concisus

What is the species taxon ID for C. concisus?




 ✗11485


 ✓199


 ✗33237











Why?





Every taxon in the NCBI Taxonomy Database has one stable, unique numeric ID; for Campylobacter concisus it’s 199. The other numbers belong to different taxa, so pinning down the taxon ID first is what lets you search every linked database for exactly the right species.



Once we know how the databases refer to the exact species we are interested in, we can find data for that species uniquely and accurately.
Use the taxid as input at ENA to find assemblies. Assemblies are the result of combining sequence reads in order to get a whole genome sequence. For bacteria the process can often result in a complete, unfragmented genome sequence. We will search ENA for C. concisus genome assemblies.
Use the Query Builder (Advanced Search) https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ebisearch/querybuilder.ebi to build a query that will:
1. Search for assemblies
2. Use a TAXON ID that corresponds to the one you found above

How many Assemblies are retrieved?




 ✓about 250


 ✗about 32,000


 ✗about 20











Why?





Searching ENA by taxon ID returns every assembly deposited for that species, which for C. concisus is on the order of a couple of hundred. The much larger count would sweep in records beyond a single species, and the tiny count would miss most of what’s been deposited. Note that this is a live count that grows over time, so the exact number will drift.




What is the genome length of the assembly with Accession GCA_015679965?




 ✗1861371 Mbp


 ✗15679965 bp


 ✓approximately 1.8 Mbp











Why?





The assembly record reports a total length of roughly 1.8 million bases, which is a typical size for a Campylobacter genome. “1861371 Mbp” confuses bases with megabases (that many megabases would be an absurdly huge genome), and “15679965 bp” just echoes digits from the accession rather than the reported length.



As an assembly comes from combining the raw sequence reads into a larger contiguous sequence the raw reads are held on a database too. The set of reads are grouped into an object called a Study and the Assembly belongs to that as part of the output of the Study. Explore the page and it’s links to answer this…

What parent ENA study did this assembly come from?




 ✗SAMN05908145


 ✓PRJNA348396


 ✗GCA_015679965.1











Why?





Study (project) accessions carry the PRJ prefix, so PRJNA348396 is the parent study. SAMN05908145 is a BioSample accession (the biological source material) and GCA_015679965.1 is the assembly accession itself — related records, but not the study the assembly belongs to.



Find information about the study either by starting a new search at the query builder at ENA with the Study ID you found. Or click through if the hyperlink is active.
The Study will contain lots of things like the sample used, the experimental description, and the runs of the sequencing machine. These all contain important metadata about how the reads were created. Explore the table to answer…

What sort of sequencing was performed for the experiment?




 ✓Mostly Illumina Paired End


 ✗Mostly Sanger


 ✗Only PacBio











Why?





The run metadata records the sequencing platform and library layout; here the runs are mostly Illumina paired-end, the standard short-read approach for this kind of bacterial genome project. Sanger and PacBio are different technologies that the metadata doesn’t list for this study.




How many runs appear to have gone into the assembly?




 ✗5


 ✗199


 ✓32











Why?





The study’s run table lists each sequencing run separately, and counting them gives 32. The other options are unrelated numbers (199 is the taxon ID), so it always pays to read the run count straight from the study record rather than guessing.
crumbs: 2  Genomic databases and genome assembly

title: 2  Genomic databases and genome assembly
text:
2.4 Downloading data with SRAtools
The web page provides a download button for the data, and while for this small genome with small files (relatively speaking) downloading through the browser might work, we can do this better using the SRAtools package on the command-line. The SRAtools helps downloading data from the Sequence Nucleotide Archive, a primary database like the ENA.
Start up your terminal program. We can use the SRAtools package to retrieve data in one steps. We use the fastqdump command to prepare the data and download it to an uncompressed text file in fastq format. Note that often you will not want uncompressed data as raw read data can be very large. You can check the SRAtools manuals for more flexible download options here
Download the reads for just one run - with ID SRR4434292 as follows:
fastq-dump SRR4434292

How many reads were downloaded?




 ✗399


 ✗428


 ✓399,928











Why?





When fastq-dump finishes it reports how many spots (reads) it wrote, which for this run is 399,928. The smaller numbers are just truncated fragments of that figure — the tool’s own summary line is the reliable source.




How many files were downloaded?




 ✓1


 ✗2


 ✗399,928











Why?





Run plain fastq-dump without the --split-files option and all the reads land in a single fastq file, so you get one file. You’d only get two files if you asked it to split paired mates into separate forward and reverse files.




How much (data in Megabytes) was downloaded?




 ✓about 51


 ✗about 400


 ✗399,928











Why?





The uncompressed fastq for this run is roughly 51 MB on disk — a reasonable size given ~400,000 short reads plus their quality strings. The 399,928 figure is the read count, not a file size, which is a common thing to mix up.
crumbs: 2  Genomic databases and genome assembly

title: 2  Genomic databases and genome assembly
text:
2.5 Assembling the reads with SPAdes
We can now perform an assembly of the reads, just to finish our pipeline off. The attempt here is just for completeness, we won’t get a good assembly because we’re using a restricted set of reads and a small computer.
We’ll use the SPAdes assembler https://github.com/ablab/spades, which will run with a simple command line like this:
spades.py --only-assembler --12 SRR4434292.fastq -o test_assembly
The command line specifies that we should run SPAdes using the assembler parts only (--only-assembler) and that the paired end reads are all in one file (--12), namely the fastq we downloaded from SRA. The output (-o) should go in a folder called test_assembly.
Run the command line and be prepared to wait. It takes about 10 minutes to complete. When it does, check the number of assembled scaffolds using the count (-c) facility of grep.
grep -c '>' test_assembly/scaffolds.fasta

How many scaffolds were the reads assembled into




 ✗39


 ✗42


 ✓about 65











Why?





grep -c '>' counts the FASTA header lines, and each header marks one scaffold, so the count of > lines is the number of scaffolds — about 65 here. A well-resolved bacterial assembly would have far fewer, but our restricted read set can only be pieced together into these many fragments.
crumbs: 2  Genomic databases and genome assembly

title: 2  Genomic databases and genome assembly
text:
2.6 Annotation of scaffolds with prokka
Once we have a genome assembly, we will want to know what the genes in it are. There are numerous pipelines we can use to do this, certainly for prokaryotes like our C. concisus test here. One good one is prokka https://github.com/tseemann/prokka which can give us a nice fast and generally accurate annotation over our contigs.
Running prokka in basic mode goes like this - just give it the scaffold.fasta file from the assembly.
prokka test_assembly/scaffolds.fasta
If it complains about ID lengths, try this variant.
prokka --centre X --compliant --force test_assembly/scaffolds.fasta
After about 5 minutes it will report it is finished. A timestamped set of files in a timestamped folder will be created, e.g PROKKA_06242021/PROKKA_06242021.txt.
The file PROKKA_06242021/PROKKA_06242021.txt contains a summary of the annotation. Here is the contents of this one:
organism: Genus species strain
contigs: 68
bases: 1923495
CDS: 1905
rRNA: 2
tRNA: 38
tmRNA: 1

How many protein Coding Sequences were found?




 ✓1905


 ✗68


 ✗38











Why?





Protein coding sequences are the CDS line of the prokka summary, which reads 1905. The 68 is the number of contigs and 38 is the tRNA count — both are in the same summary, so read the row label carefully to pick out the coding sequences.



The rest of the files in the folder contain various aspects of an annotation
1. <timestamp>.ffn = transcript sequences
2. <timestamp>.gff = annotations, gene descriptions, coordinates on genome
3. <timestamp>.faa = protein sequences.
crumbs: 2  Genomic databases and genome assembly

title: 2  Genomic databases and genome assembly
text:
2.7 Assessing the annotation with BUSCO
Once we have constructed an annotation we naturally would like to know how complete it is. We can assess that by seeing how many of the conserved genes in that lineage we have been able to reconstruct, that is to ask the question ‘how many of the genes that should be in our genome did we find?’ We can do this using the tool BUSCO https://busco.ezlab.org/busco_userguide.html. BUSCO has a set of conserved genes that it calls BUSCO GROUPS for each lineage. It will search and tell you how many it found and how many it was expecting. The higher a proportion your analysis finds, the better the assembly and annotation is.
BUSCO needs our transcript sequences and the lineage we want to assess them in - C. concisus is an epsilon proteobacterium, so we can use that (the manual for BUSCO shows you how to work out what lineages it can use). Then we simply pass BUSCO the input file (-i) and the lineage tag (-l) and a folder to put the results in (-o) along with telling it to work in transcriptome mode (-m transcriptome)
For the timestamped prokka annotation run above the command looks like this
busco -i PROKKA_06242021/PROKKA_06242021.ffn -l epsilonproteobacteria_odb10 -o busco_out -m transcriptome
Do the same command for your own prokka run. REMEMBER TO CHANGE THE TIMESTAMP TO THE ONE TODAY The analysis should take about 5 minutes. The summary results go into a file called busco_out/short_summary.specific.epsilonproteobacteria_odb10.busco_out.txt.
Inspect the file, when it should look something like this

    --------------------------------------------------
    |Results from dataset epsilonproteobacteria_odb10 |
    --------------------------------------------------
    |C:89.8%[S:89.8%,D:0.0%],F:0.2%,M:10.0%,n:591     |
    |531    Complete BUSCOs (C)                         |
    |531    Complete and single-copy BUSCOs (S)         |
    |0  Complete and duplicated BUSCOs (D)            |
    |1  Fragmented BUSCOs (F)                         |
    |59 Missing BUSCOs (M)                            |
    |591    Total BUSCO groups searched                 |
    --------------------------------------------------


How many BUSCOs (conserved genes) did the program expect to find?




 ✗531


 ✗59


 ✓591











Why?





The “Total BUSCO groups searched” line (n:591) is the number of conserved genes BUSCO expected for the epsilonproteobacteria lineage — the denominator for completeness. 531 is how many it actually found and 59 is how many were missing.




How many BUSCOs (conserved genes) did the program actually find?




 ✓531


 ✗59


 ✗591











Why?





The “Complete BUSCOs (C)” line reports 531 genes recovered from the 591 that were searched for. 59 is the missing count and 591 is the total expected, so the found figure is the one on the Complete line.




This is a good result, right?




 ✓Yes


 ✗No











Why?





Recovering 531 of 591 conserved genes is a high completeness score, which is a good outcome given we assembled from a deliberately small read set. Higher completeness means more of the genes that ought to be present were actually reconstructed.



The result 531 / 591 BUSCOs found (89.85% ) is not bad - considering we assembled a genome from quite a small number of reads. For real use cases we might hope for a lot better from prokaryote data. For large plant genomes numbers might be similar.
crumbs: 2  Genomic databases and genome assembly

title: 2  Genomic databases and genome assembly
text:
2.8 Wrap up
And there we complete a full experiment, using public databases to identify a species of interest, download some reads for it and assemble them using a genome assembler. Then we annotated the assembly to find genes and assessed the set of genes we did find to see how many of the ones we expected to be there were in fact there.
crumbs: 2  Genomic databases and genome assembly

title: 3  Finding structures and structural motifs using databases
text:
3.1 Aims
The aim of this tutorial is to examine how we can use public tools and databases to retrieve structures and predict the structures of our own protein of interest. We shall then work through an example in which we search for a known structure that fits a protein of interest and predict the structure of a protein of our own.
crumbs: 3  Finding structures and structural motifs using databases

title: 3  Finding structures and structural motifs using databases
section: 3.2 Browsing for structural data at PDBe
text:
3.2 Browsing for structural data at PDBe
Protein structure databases contain data that describes the physical 3D structure of proteins that has (usually) been experimentally determined. The central archive is the Protein Data Bank and it informs the content of other specialised databases. The structural data format (.pdb) that is used is complicated and not as widely used by life scientists as the sequence formats like FASTA.
The best way to search for structural data is to use the specific tools provided at PDBe https://www.ebi.ac.uk/pdbe/. A typical analysis will start with a query sequence of interest derived from earlier experiments.
We’ll use a sample input protein and work through finding a structure that fits it closely. The protein is APG8A, from Arabidopsis, a Ubiquitin-like superfamily protein.
>protein
MAKSSFKISNPLEARMSESSRIREKYPDRIPVIVEKA
GQSDVPDIDKKKYLVPADLTVGQFVYVVRKRIKLGAE
KAIFVFVKNTLPPTAALMSAIYEEHKDEDGFLYMTYS
GENTFGSLTVA
Start PDBe sequence Search at https://www.ebi.ac.uk/pdbe and in the Advanced Search Service select a new FASTA sequence search and paste the protein sequence above into the box that appears (be careful to not include the name). This procedure uses standard sequence similarity searches, that is to say similar amino acid sequences are used to find match to sequences that have structural data in the database. No structural data is used in the match itself. The matches that are made have sequence similarity and structural similarity is inferred.

What is the PDBe database ID of the best hit?




 ✗M1C146


 ✓5l83


 ✗27458016











Why?





The PDBe entry ID 5l83 is a four-character PDB accession code — the standard format for a deposited structure. M1C146 looks like a UniProt sequence accession and 27458016 like a numeric database identifier, so neither points to a structure in the PDB.




What is the actual structure that was found?




 ✗A potato homologue of APG8A


 ✗The potato blight effector protein PexRD54


 ✓The potato ATG8 protein in complex with PexRD54











Why?





The search matched our ATG8-like query to a deposited structure of the potato ATG8 protein bound to the Phytophthora infestans effector PexRD54. Because the entry is a complex, it contains both proteins together — not the ATG8 or the effector on its own.



Click the picture to see the 3D visualisation of the best hit.

How many helices can be seen in the larger of the two molecules in the dimer?




 ✓2


 ✗3


 ✗4











Why?





Rotating the 3D view of the larger molecule shows two helices packed against the central beta sheet — the classic ubiquitin-like fold that ATG8 adopts.




Which amino acid is at the C terminal end of the C terminal Beta sheet?




 ✗Glutamine


 ✓Serine


 ✗Lysine











Why?





Following the final beta strand to its C-terminal end in the 3D viewer lands on a serine residue. The viewer lets you hover over individual residues to confirm the amino acid identity at any position.



Go back to the search results and click the download files link. View the PDB file.

What sort of record makes up most of the file?




 ✗REMARK


 ✗SITE


 ✓ATOM











Why?





A PDB file stores one line per atom, so ATOM records vastly outnumber every other record type. REMARK and SITE lines carry metadata and annotations, of which there are only a handful.



Examine the Wikipedia entry for the PDB format. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protein_Data_Bank_(file_format)

What does the ATOM record describe?




 ✓the coordinates of the atoms that are part of the protein


 ✗the researcher who defined the structure


 ✗the sequences of the peptides











Why?





Each ATOM record gives the x, y and z coordinates of a single atom in the structure — the raw geometry from which the 3D model is built. The other details, like who solved the structure, live in header records such as AUTHOR, not in ATOM.
crumbs: 3  Finding structures and structural motifs using databases

title: 3  Finding structures and structural motifs using databases
text:
3.3 Using Phyre2 for structural analysis
The Phyre2 program predicts 3 dimensional structures using homology modelling. In this approach the protein sequence of interest is used as a basis to construct a hypothetical structure model based on the atoms of the protein. Structures in that model are used to inform and improve the sequence level alignment and allow for more distant matches and matches based on the physical structure.
Here is the protein sequence again.
>protein
MAKSSFKISNPLEARMSESSRIREKYPDRIPVIVEKA
GQSDVPDIDKKKYLVPADLTVGQFVYVVRKRIKLGAE
KAIFVFVKNTLPPTAALMSAIYEEHKDEDGFLYMTYS
GENTFGSLTVA
Go to the Phyre2 homepage http://www.sbg.bio.ic.ac.uk/phyre2/html/page.cgi?id=index and paste the sequence in to the job page. Homology modelling takes a long time to do, so you’ll need to leave an email address to retrieve the results later. A previous run can be seen at https://danmaclean.github.io/phyreresults/ (some features of the webpage won’t work because it isn’t on the Phyre2 server, wait for that for absolutely everything)

What is the percent sequence identity of the top hit (d3d32a1)?




 ✗100


 ✗94


 ✓57











Why?





The top hit shares only 57% of its sequence with our query, yet homology modelling can still return a high-confidence match. That’s the point of the method — mapping the physical fold lets it recognise relatives that sequence identity alone would call distant.



The sequence identity in these models can be quite low relative to the PDBe method while the confidence in the hit can be quite high. This is because the structural feature mapping is powerful. Click the alignment button to see the secondary structure feature alignment.

How many predicted sheets and helices does the input query sequence have that the known structure does not?




 ✓0


 ✗1


 ✗2











Why?





Every predicted sheet and helix in our query lines up with one in the known structure, so the count of extra features is zero. That close correspondence of secondary structure is exactly what gives us confidence the model is a good one.



Download the 3D model for the best hit (d3d32a1) in PDB format from this page.
crumbs: 3  Finding structures and structural motifs using databases

title: 3  Finding structures and structural motifs using databases
text:
3.4 Visualising structural models of proteins
Along the way we’ve seen numerous 3D renderings of proteins and secondary structure alignments, but we haven’t seen a 3D alignment of the hits and our query protein fragment. To achieve that we need a PDB file of the hit from the database (which we downloaded in the last step). We also need a PDB file from our original protein of interest, which we don’t have. We can use an ab initio protein modeller to do that. These are computationally quite intensive so take a long time and require sign up, as an example there’s one at https://robetta.bakerlab.org/. Go there and sign up.
When you’ve signed up and are on the home page, you can choose the Submit option from the Structure Prediction menu to submit a sequence for structural prediction. On that page you can see the upload boxes and some options for the modelling process. When you press the Submit button at the bottom, the sequence is added to the processing queue. It can take a long time to work through the sequence and build a predicted model, several hours in most cases! So don’t actually run the sequence this time! Once the modelling is run the program returns a link and the PDB file to your email address. Here is a pre-computed model file for our protein of interest, https://raw.githubusercontent.com/danmaclean/phyreresults/main/robetta_models_89846.pdb
You can download a large text file like this most easily by using wget on the command-line, like this
wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/danmaclean/phyreresults/main/robetta_models_89846.pdb
Now we have a predicted model of our protein of interest we can align it with the model of the best hit (d3d32a1). The alignment can be done with a program called TM-align at https://zhanglab.ccmb.med.umich.edu/TM-align/. The web form here is straightforward to use. Upload the model from Robetta as protein 1 and the best hit from Phyre d3d32a1 as protein 2. Then hit align. In a short while your alignment should appear

Is the alignment a good one? Do the protein and the best hit match structures well?




 ✓Yes


 ✗No











Why?





The two structures superpose closely in 3D, so the alignment is a good one. A strong structural overlap like this is what lets us infer the shape of our protein from a known relative.




TM-score is a measure of how similar the structures are. What is the TM-score for this alignment?




 ✗122/155


 ✓0.87/0.92











Why?





The TM-score is reported as a value between 0 and 1 (here normalised two ways, by the length of each protein), so 0.87/0.92 is the score. 122/155 is the count of aligned residues, not the TM-score. Scores above ~0.5 indicate the two proteins share the same fold.




What is TM-align’s suggested upper bound on TM-score for a random alignment?




 ✗0.0


 ✓0.3


 ✗0.5


 ✗1.0











Why?





Two unrelated structures scored at random top out around 0.3, so anything comfortably above that signals real structural similarity. Our score of ~0.9 is far beyond this threshold, confirming the match is genuine rather than chance.
crumbs: 3  Finding structures and structural motifs using databases

title: 3  Finding structures and structural motifs using databases
text:
3.5 Wrap up
In this tutorial we’ve investigated a pipeline for finding a potential structure of a protein sequence of interest that we have no structural information about. We used sequence similarity and homology modeling to find similar known structures. We then predicted a structure ab initio for our sequence of interest and used that to align in 3D space with our best match protein. In this way we have been able to infer the structure of our protein of interest.
crumbs: 3  Finding structures and structural motifs using databases
